title: WVU physicists discover how electron interactions shape materials important for quantum computing | E-News | West Virginia University
why:
- Researchers discovered that adjusting the ratio of elements in iron telluride selenide allows switching “exotic” quantum states on and off in materials important for quantum computing.
- Iron telluride selenide possesses superconductivity and exotic properties valuable for quantum computing and is typically grown in ultra-thin films.
- By changing interactions between electrons in these films, scientists can “tune” the material between different quantum phases, reaching a highly desirable state called a “topological superconductor.”
- Topological superconductors are promising for building error-free quantum devices because they are inherently stable and resistant to noise.
- The study revealed that altering the ratio of tellurium and selenium changes the correlations between electrons, which serves as a sensitive control for engineering exotic quantum phases.
